title: Unit Economics & Business Cases for Staff+ Engineers
subtitle: How to speak the language of ROI, COGS, and margin — and why technical decisions at FAANG are business decisions first

sections.content:
## Why Staff+ Engineers Must Understand Unit Economics

At SAP, technical decisions are often made in isolation: "We need more HANA memory for better performance" or "This refactoring will take 3 months." Budget approval is a formality.

At FAANG, every technical decision is a **business decision**. A Staff+ engineer's proposal competes with every other proposal for the same engineering budget. The engineer who can frame their proposal in business terms — ROI, COGS reduction, margin improvement, customer LTV impact — gets funded. The engineer who only talks about technical benefits doesn't.

This article covers the unit economics frameworks every Staff+ engineer needs to:
1. **Build business cases** that get funded
2. **Evaluate tradeoffs** between cost and performance
3. **Communicate with executives** in their language
4. **Understand why the business makes the decisions it does**

### The Staff+ Gap

| Dimension | SAP Engineer | FAANG Staff+ Engineer |
|---|---|---|
| Budget ask | 'We need more hardware' | 'This reduces infrastructure COGS by 15%, saving $2M/year' |
| Refactoring | 'This improves code quality' | 'This reduces onboarding time by 40%, saving $500K in ramp-up costs' |
| New system | 'Use the latest tech' | 'This reduces per-request cost by 60%, enabling 3x throughput at same infra cost' |
| Staffing ask | 'We need more engineers' | 'Adding 2 engineers reduces contractor spend by $400K and improves velocity by 30%' |

cards.title: Unit Economics 101
cards.body:
**Unit economics** is the revenue and cost per unit of business. For a SaaS product:

- **LTV (Lifetime Value)** — Total revenue from a customer over their lifetime
- **CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost)** — Cost to acquire a customer
- **Gross Margin** — (Revenue - COGS) / Revenue
- **Payback Period** — CAC / (Monthly Revenue - Monthly COGS)

For infrastructure teams, the 'unit' is different:
- **Per-request cost** — Total infra cost / Request count
- **Per-transaction cost** — Total processing cost / Transaction count
- **Per-storage GB cost** — Total storage cost / Data volume

**Rule of thumb:** A healthy SaaS business has LTV:CAC > 3, payback period < 12 months, gross margin > 70%.

cards.title: COGS Reduction vs Revenue Generation
cards.body:
Engineering proposals fall into two categories:

**COGS Reduction** (cost savings):
- Reduce cloud spend (compute, storage, bandwidth)
- Reduce operational burden (on-call, manual processes)
- Reduce vendor licensing costs
- Eliminate technical debt that slows development

**Revenue Generation** (growth enablement):
- Enable new features that increase conversion
- Reduce latency to improve user retention (every 100ms = +1% revenue at Amazon scale)
- Increase platform reliability to prevent revenue loss during outages
- Enable self-serve provisioning to reduce sales cycle

**FAANG bias:** Revenue generation proposals get funded more easily. If you can tie your project to a revenue metric, you will almost always win over a pure cost-savings proposal of similar magnitude.

cards.title: TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)
cards.body:
When evaluating build vs buy or migration decisions:

**TCO = Acquisition + Operation + Maintenance + Decommission**

**Example: Migrating from self-managed Kafka to MSK**
- Acquisition: $0 (both are software), but migration takes 3 engineer-months ($120K)
- Operation: Self-managed costs 0.5 engineer/week ($52K/year) plus EC2 ($24K/year) = $76K/year
- MSK: Fully managed, $30K/year + 0.05 engineer/week ($5K/year) = $35K/year
- Maintenance: Self-managed Kafka upgrades take 2 weeks/year ($40K); MSK upgrades are automatic
- 3-year TCO: Self-managed = $120K + 3*$76K = $348K. MSK = $120K + 3*$35K = $225K
- **Savings: $123K over 3 years (35%)**

cards.title: The Business Case Template
cards.body:
**Executive Summary** (2-3 sentences): What, why, how much?
**Problem Statement:** Current state quantified (cost, latency, reliability gap)
**Proposed Solution:** Technical approach (2-3 options with tradeoffs)
**Financial Impact:**
- Investment: Engineering time + infra cost
- Benefits: Cost savings OR revenue impact (with confidence range)
- Payback period: Months to recover investment
- NPV/ROI: Net present value over 3 years
**Risks:** What could go wrong and mitigation
**Dependencies:** Who needs to approve/deliver what
**Timeline:** Phases with milestone dates
**Recommendation:** Which option and why

sections.title: Infrastructure Cost Models at FAANG Scale
rows: Spot instances (60-90% discount), rightsizing, ARM (Graviton) 40% cheaper
Autoscaling + spot = 50-70% savings on batch workloads
Lifecycle policies (hot -> warm -> cold -> archive), erasure coding (1.3x vs 3x replication)
Archive old data = 90% storage cost reduction
Read replicas for analytics, DynamoDB auto-scaling, reserved capacity
CDN caching, multi-AZ within region (no cross-region egress), compression
Reduce cross-region traffic = 80% network cost reduction
Topic compaction, log retention tuning, partition count optimization
Tune retention from 7 days to 3 days = 57% storage cost reduction
Spot GPU, preemptible TPUs, mixed-precision training (2x speedup)
Spot + mixed precision = 60-80% training cost reduction

sections.title: Business Case Example: Caching Layer Migration
sections.code:
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY:
Migrate from self-managed Redis to Amazon ElastiCache Serverless
for the user session store. Reduces operational burden by 2 engineers/
quarter and infrastructure cost by 35%. Total 3-year savings: $420K.
Payback period: 4 months.

PROBLEM:
- Current Redis cluster (6 nodes, r6g.xlarge) costs $1,728/month
- Requires 0.5 engineer/week for patching, monitoring, failover testing
- P99 latency has degraded from 2ms to 8ms as data grows
- Scaling requires manual shard rebalancing (4-hour maintenance window)

PROPOSED SOLUTION: Amazon ElastiCache Serverless
- No cluster management
- Auto-scales from 1GB to 256GB without reconfiguration
- P99 latency < 1ms (Dedicated Nitro-based infrastructure)
- Cost: ~$1,120/month (pay-per-request + per-GB-hour)

FINANCIAL IMPACT:
| Item | Current | Proposed | Annual Savings |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infrastructure | $20,736/yr | $13,440/yr | $7,296 |
| Engineering (patching) | $26,000/yr | $2,600/yr | $23,400 |
| Engineering (on-call) | $15,600/yr | $5,200/yr | $10,400 |
| Scaling operations | $10,400/yr | $0/yr | $10,400 |
| TOTAL | $72,736/yr | $21,240/yr | $51,496/yr (71%) |

Migration effort: 6 engineer-weeks ($48,000 one-time)
Payback: $48,000 / $4,291/month = 11.2 months
3-year ROI: (3 * $51,496 - $48,000) / $48,000 = 222%

RISKS:
- Migration downtime: Rollback plan tested, max 15-min cutover
- Redis module compatibility: Verify GeoSets usage before migration

RECOMMENDATION: Proceed. Low risk, high ROI, immediate operational relief.

sections.title: Business Case FAQ for Staff+ Interviews

tabs.label: Build the Case
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Build a business case for moving from monolith to microservices."

**Staff+ answer:** "I'll frame this around **developer velocity** and **operational cost**, not just 'modern architecture.'

**Problem:** The monolith takes 45 minutes to build. Deploy frequency is twice per week. Each deploy has a 15% chance of regression because changes can't be isolated. Annualized: 104 deploys x 15% = 15 regressions, each averaging 4 engineer-hours to diagnose = 60 hours/year lost.

**Proposal:** Extract the payment processing module as a microservice. Investment: 2 teams x 3 months = $360K engineering cost.

**Benefits:**
- Payment service deploys independently: deploy frequency goes from 2/week to 20/week
- Regressions isolated to payment domain: 5% chance instead of 15% = 48 hours saved/year
- Payment team velocity: 3x improvement (no monolith build wait)
- Infrastructure: payment service can scale independently, reducing over-provisioning by 40%

**Financial:** Investment $360K, annual savings $120K (engineering time + infra). Payback: 3 years.

**But the bigger win is velocity-driven revenue.** If the payment team ships 3x faster, and each new payment feature increases conversion by 0.5%, and the company processes $1B/year, that's $5M incremental revenue. The business case shifts from cost savings to revenue enablement."

**Why this impresses:** You quantified both cost savings AND revenue impact. You gave concrete numbers (45min build, 15% regression, $5M revenue). You showed you understand that business cases are about tradeoffs, not absolutes.

tabs.label: Tradeoff Quantification
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Your team wants to rewrite an internal dashboard. The rewrite takes 6 months. Build the business case."

**Staff+ answer:** "First, I need to understand the current cost of NOT rewriting:

**Current State:**
- Dashboard crashes 3x/week, each crash takes 30 minutes to recover = 1.5 hours/week lost productivity
- 200 users x 1.5 hours/week x $150/hour burdened cost = $45K/week = $2.3M/year in lost productivity
- New features take 4 weeks to ship (the code is unmaintainable)
- User satisfaction NPS: -20

**Proposal:** Rewrite using React + real-time data pipeline. 6 months, 4 engineers = $480K investment.

**Benefits:**
- Zero crashes (modern architecture) = save $2.3M/year
- Feature velocity: 1 week instead of 4 = 4x faster
- NPS improvement from -20 to +40 (hard to quantify, but retention impact is real)

**Financial:**
- Year 1: -$480K (investment) + $1.15M (6 months of crash savings) = +$670K
- Year 2+: $2.3M/year savings
- Payback: ~2.5 months
- 3-year ROI: ($2.3M*2.5 - $480K) / $480K = 1096%

**Risk:** 6-month rewrite might slip. Mitigation: phased rollout, core dashboard first (3 months), advanced features second."

**Why this impresses:** You calculated the cost of NOT doing the project (crash cost). This is the most overlooked and most powerful number in a business case. The 1096% ROI makes the decision obvious.

tabs.label: SAP Context
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "You want to migrate from SAP ECC to S/4HANA. Build the business case using FAANG unit economics."

**Staff+ answer:** "At FAANG, we'd frame this around three metrics:

**1. Infrastructure cost reduction:**
- ECC on legacy DB: $500K/year in DB licensing + $200K in hardware
- S/4HANA on HANA Cloud: $600K/year (HANA Cloud compute) + $100K (BYOL amortized)
- Net: $0 difference (HANA is not cheaper)

**2. Operational efficiency (COGS):**
- Current: 5 DBAs at $200K/year each to manage ECC + legacy DB = $1M/year
- S/4HANA: 2 DBAs (HANA is simpler) = $400K/year
- Annual savings: $600K
- But this requires retraining or headcount reduction

**3. Revenue enablement (the FAANG framing):**
- Current state: Month-end close takes 10 days. Each day delay in financial reporting costs $100K in working capital inefficiency (estimated).
- S/4HANA: Real-time close in 2 days (8 days faster).
- Annual benefit: 8 days x 12 months x $100K = $9.6M

**Total 3-year business case:**
- Investment: $2M (migration + training)
- Savings: $600K/year ops + $9.6M/year revenue benefit = $10.2M/year
- Payback: ~2.4 months
- 3-year ROI: ($30.6M - $2M) / $2M = 1430%

**Meta-point:** The revenue enablement number dominates. Most SAP business cases focus on 'reducing TCO' (which is marginal). The real value is enabling faster business decisions."

**Why this impresses:** You showed you can apply FAANG-style unit economics (COGS, revenue enablement) to SAP projects. You identified that the real ROI is in business speed, not IT cost reduction.

tabs.label: Executive Pitch
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "You have 30 seconds with the VP. What do you say?"

**Staff+ answer:** "'VP, our payment processing pipeline currently costs $2.5M/year and breaks 3 times per quarter. I have a plan to make it zero-maintenance, auto-scaling, and reduce cost by 60%. Investment: $400K and 4 months. Payback: 8 months. After that, $1.5M/year savings plus we eliminate the pager duty that's burning out the team. Can I share the one-pager?'

**Why this works:** Headline ($2.5M -> $1M), timeline (4 months), payback (8 months), and a secondary benefit (team burnout). The VP can decide in 10 seconds whether to give you 30 more minutes."

**Key principles for executive pitches:**
1. Lead with the dollar impact
2. State investment + timeline upfront
3. Give a hard number for the benefit (not 'improved efficiency')
4. Include a secondary benefit (retention, velocity, risk reduction)
5. End with an ask ('Can I share the one-pager?')
6. **Never** start with technical details (architecture, technology choices)

**Why this impresses:** You gave a concrete, rehearsable pitch that hits every executive communication principle. This is the difference between a Staff+ engineer and a Principal engineer.

cards.title: Every Decision Is a Business Decision
cards.body: FAANG funds projects based on ROI, not technical merit. Frame every proposal in business terms: cost savings, revenue enablement, or risk reduction.

cards.title: Revenue Wins Over Cost
cards.body: Revenue generation proposals are funded 5x more than cost savings. If your project can be tied to a revenue metric, lead with that number.

cards.title: Payback Period Is Critical
cards.body: A <12-month payback is a green light. 12-24 months needs strong justification. >24 months rarely gets funded unless it's a strategic bet.

cards.title: Quantify the Current Cost
cards.body: The most powerful number in a business case is the cost of NOT doing the project. Measure crashes, inefficiency, lost productivity.

cards.title: Include a Confidence Range
cards.body: No business case is certain. Give a range: '$1-2M savings (P50-P90).' This shows you understand uncertainty and builds trust.

cards.title: 30-Second Pitch Ready
cards.body: Always have a 30-second version of your business case ready. If you can't explain the value in 30 seconds, you don't understand it well enough.
